findings.description: SQL injection: the email parameter is interpolated directly into the query string via an f-string, allowing authentication bypass and data exfiltration.
Inverted expiry check: 'expires_at > now' deletes and rejects still-valid sessions while letting already-expired sessions through, breaking all session validation.
Because of the inverted check, expired session tokens are accepted and return a valid user id, so sessions never actually expire from the caller's perspective.
find_user_by_email loads every row from the users table and linearly scans in Python instead of filtering with a WHERE clause, an O(n) full-table scan.
Lockout never expires: LOCKOUT_MINUTES is defined but unused and failed_attempts is only reset on successful login, so a locked account stays permanently locked (DoS / no time-based unlock).
User-enumeration timing side channel: an unknown email returns immediately while a known email performs the expensive PBKDF2 hash, leaking account existence via response time.
datetime.utcnow() is deprecated in Python 3.12+ and returns naive datetimes, risking incorrect comparisons across timezone-aware code and future breakage.
Failed-attempt counter has no upper bound or cap and increments unconditionally, enabling an attacker to lock out arbitrary known accounts by submitting wrong passwords (lockout-as-DoS).
In-memory _sessions dict is mutated without locking; concurrent requests (threaded server) can race on del/pop/insert leading to KeyError or lost sessions.
PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA256 with only 100,000 iterations is below current OWASP guidance (~600k+), weakening resistance to offline brute-force.
Sessions are stored only in memory, so all active sessions are lost on process restart and cannot be shared across multiple instances.
DB writes (failed-attempt increment / reset) have no exception handling; a commit failure propagates uncaught and may leave attempt counts inconsistent.
is_active is always 1 because the query filters 'is_active = 1', so bool(row['is_active']) is a redundant always-true conversion and the field is effectively dead.
Logging the user id on lockout/expiry events is minor information disclosure to log readers and ties identifiers to security events.
validate_session mutates shared state (deletes the session) as a side effect of a read-style 'validate' call, which is surprising and compounds the inverted-check bug.
_hash_password lacks a docstring and there is no documented password/salt length or encoding contract for the security-critical hashing routine.
